corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: recent methods for learning vector space representations of words have succeeded in capturing fine-grained semantic and syntactic regularities using vector arithmetic but the origin of these regularities has remained opaque we analyze and make explicit the model properties needed for such regularities to emerge in word vectors the result is a new global logbilinear regression model that combines the advantages of the two major model families in the literature global matrix factorization and local context window methods our model efficiently leverages statistical information by training only on the nonzero elements in a word-word cooccurrence matrix rather than on the entire sparse matrix or on individual context windows in a large corpus the model produces a vector space with meaningful substructure as evidenced by its performance of 75 on a recent word analogy task it also outperforms related models on similarity tasks and named entity recognition body

top_candidates.sentence: GloVe outperforms it while using a corpus less than half the size.
The GloVe model outperforms all other methods on all evaluation metrics, except for the CoNLL test set, on which the HPCA method does slightly better.
9 For the same corpus, vocabulary, window size, and training time, GloVe consistently outperforms word2vec.
It achieves better results faster, and also obtains the best results irrespective of speed.

For example, introduced a model that learns word vector representations as part of a simple neural network architecture for language modeling. decoupled the word vector training from the downstream training objectives, which paved the way for to use the full context of a word for learning the word representations, rather than just the preceding context as is the case with language models.

The skip-gram and continuous bag-of-words (CBOW) models of propose a simple single-layer architecture based on the inner product between two word vectors. also proposed closely-related vector log-bilinear models, vLBL and ivLBL, and proposed explicit word embeddings based on a PPMI metric.

The skip-gram and continuous bag-of-words (CBOW) models of propose a simple single-layer architecture based on the inner product between two word vectors. also proposed closely-related vector log-bilinear models, vLBL and ivLBL, and proposed explicit word embeddings based on a PPMI metric.
First, we would like F to encode the information present the ratio P ik /P j k in the word vector space.
We propose a new weighted least squares regression model that addresses these problems.
To allow for efficient training, the skip-gram and ivLBL models introduce approximations to Q i j .
With this in mind, we introduce a more general weighting function, which we are free to take to depend on the context word as well.
We conduct experiments on the word analogy task of , a variety of word similarity tasks, as described in , and on the CoNLL-2003 shared benchmark Word analogies.
The word analogy task consists of questions like, "a is to b as c is to ?" The dataset contains 19,544 such questions, divided into a semantic subset and a syntactic subset.
The CoNLL-2003 English benchmark dataset for NER is a collection of documents from Reuters newswire articles, annotated with four entity types: person, location, organization, and miscellaneous.
The CoNLL-2003 English benchmark dataset for NER is a collection of documents from Reuters newswire articles, annotated with four entity types: person, location, organization, and miscellaneous.
A total of 437,905 discrete features were generated for the CoNLL-2003 training dataset.

This is due to a number of factors, including our choice to use negative sampling (which typically works better than the hierarchical softmax), the number of negative samples, and the choice of the corpus.
We demonstrate that the model can easily be trained on a large 42 billion token corpus, with a substantial corresponding performance boost.

We note that increasing the corpus size does not guarantee improved results for other models, as can be seen by the decreased performance of the SVD- 65.8 72.7 77.8 53.9 38.1 SVD-L 42B 74.0 76.4 74.1 58.3 39.9 GloVe 42B 75.9 83.6 82.9 59.6 47.8 CBOW * 100B 68.4 79.6 75.4 59.4 45.5 L model on this larger corpus.
GloVe outperforms it while using a corpus less than half the size.
On the syntactic subtask, there is a monotonic increase in performance as the corpus size increases.
Interestingly, the same trend is not true for the semantic subtask, where the models trained on the smaller Wikipedia corpora do better than those trained on the larger Gigaword corpus.
This is likely due to the large number of city-and countrybased analogies in the analogy dataset and the fact that Wikipedia has fairly comprehensive articles for most such locations.

Moreover, Wikipedia's 50 55 60 65 70 75 80 85 Overall Syntactic Semantic Wiki2010 1B tokens Accuracy [%] entries are updated to assimilate new knowledge, whereas Gigaword is a fixed news repository with outdated and possibly incorrect information.
The former depends on many factors, including window size, vocabulary size, and corpus size.
